title: Forked Paths: When Information Gain Wins

runtime.expected_outcome: Agent takes the longer but more informative fork when the pragmatic term is weak; picks the shortcut once preferences dominate. Visible in EFE decomposition.

audiences.kid: Two ways to get to the goal: one is faster but you don't learn much; the other takes longer but you see a lot. The agent picks based on how hungry it is.
audiences.real: On the forked-paths maze, epistemic and pragmatic terms compete. Low preference makes the agent take the information-rich long path; high preference collapses it to the shortcut.
audiences.equation: Minimising G trades off -D_KL (epistemic value) vs. pragmatic loss -ln p(o). Same G, different weights produce different policies.
audiences.derivation: Under q(o,s|pi) = q(s|o,pi) q(o|pi), the expected log ratio simplifies to the negative KL between posterior and prior predictive beliefs. This is the information-gain component of G (Friston 2015).

orchestrate.objective: Show a preference-vs-information tradeoff in policy selection, observable in the EFE decomposition graph.
orchestrate.role: Epistemic-value tutor using the forked-paths maze as the canonical fixture.
orchestrate.context: forked_paths 7x5 maze; change preference_strength between 0.5 (epistemic wins) and 6.0 (pragmatic wins).

credits: Parr, Pezzulo & Friston (2022), Active Inference, MIT Press (CC BY-NC-ND), Ch 4.
Friston, Rigoli, Ognibene, Mathys, Fitzgerald & Pezzulo (2015), Active inference and epistemic value.
